title: AI's Use In Transfer Pricing Still Evolving, Tax Pros Say - Law360
summary: AI's Use In Transfer Pricing Still Evolving, Tax Pros Say Law360
ai_summary: Tax professionals indicate that the application of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in transfer pricing is still in its early stages of development. A Law360 article discusses the evolving landscape of how AI tools are being utilized to enhance or streamline various aspects of transfer pricing analysis and compliance.

title: Calcutta HC Orders Refund as Section 245 Adjustment Exceeded 20% of Disputed Demand
summary: The High Court held that adjustment of refunds beyond 20% of disputed tax demand during pendency of appeal was unsustainable without proper justification. It directed refund of the excess amount adjusted under Section 245 of the Income Tax Act. The post Calcutta HC Orders Refund as Section 245 Adjustment Exceeded 20% of Disputed Demand appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Calcutta High Court in India ordered a refund, ruling that an adjustment made under Section 245 of the Income Tax Act exceeded 20% of the disputed demand without proper justification. The court directed the refund of the excess amount adjusted during the pendency of an appeal.

title: ITAT Deletes Section 50C Addition as Stamp Value Difference Was Within 5% Safe Harbour
summary: The ITAT Indore held that Section 50C could not be invoked where the difference between actual sale consideration and stamp duty valuation was only 1.19%. The Tribunal directed adoption of actual sale consideration as the full value for capital gains computation. The post ITAT Deletes Section 50C Addition as Stamp Value Difference Was Within 5% Safe Harbour appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The ITAT Indore in India ruled that a Section 50C addition for deemed capital gains was unwarranted because the difference between the actual sale consideration and stamp duty valuation was only 1.19%, falling within the 5% safe harbour. The Tribunal directed that the actual sale consideration should be adopted for capital gains computation.

title: Gujarat HC Quashes Section 263 Notice as AO’s DVO-Based Valuation Was Plausible
summary: The Gujarat High Court held that revisional powers under Section 263 cannot be invoked merely because the Commissioner prefers another valuation method. The Court ruled that the Assessing Officer had conducted proper inquiry and adopted a plausible view based on the DVO report. The post Gujarat HC Quashes Section 263 Notice as AO’s DVO-Based Valuation Was Plausible appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Gujarat High Court in India quashed a Section 263 notice, ruling that the Commissioner could not invoke revisional powers merely by preferring a different valuation method. The Court affirmed that the Assessing Officer's DVO-based valuation was plausible after proper inquiry.

title: Section 69B Addition Confirmed as AO Recorded Discrepancies Before DVO Reference
summary: Madras High Court held that a reference to the District Valuation Officer was valid because the Assessing Officer had effectively rejected the books of accounts after recording discrepancies. The Court upheld the Section 69B addition for unexplained investment in building construction. The post Section 69B Addition Confirmed as AO Recorded Discrepancies Before DVO Reference appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Madras High Court in India confirmed a Section 69B addition for unexplained investment in building construction, affirming that the reference to the District Valuation Officer was valid. The Court noted that the Assessing Officer had recorded discrepancies before rejecting the books of accounts.

title: FIFO Prevails: ITAT Mumbai Rejects “Cherry-Picked Shares” as Tax Avoidance
summary: Mumbai ITAT upheld ₹10.76 crore addition after rejecting selective identification of physical shares for capital gains computation. The Tribunal termed the arrangement a “colourable device” to suppress taxable gains. The post FIFO Prevails: ITAT Mumbai Rejects “Cherry-Picked Shares” as Tax Avoidance appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Mumbai ITAT upheld a ₹10.76 crore addition, ruling that selective identification of physical shares for capital gains computation was a 'colourable device' for tax avoidance. This decision, focusing on the FIFO method, pertains to domestic capital gains taxation and is not directly related to transfer pricing.

title: Section 56 Addition Deleted as DCF Valuation Was Not Rejected: ITAT Delhi
summary: ITAT Delhi held that the Assessing Officer could not substitute the fair market value of shares without specifically rejecting the assessee’s DCF valuation report. The Tribunal deleted the Rs.4.14 crore addition made under Section 56(2)(vii)(b). The post Section 56 Addition Deleted as DCF Valuation Was Not Rejected: ITAT Delhi appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The ITAT Delhi ruled that an Assessing Officer cannot substitute the fair market value of shares without explicitly rejecting the assessee's Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) valuation report. This decision deleted a ₹4.14 crore addition under Section 56(2)(vii)(b), reinforcing the importance of proper valuation methodology and its acceptance in tax assessments.

title: Weighted Average Cost method ignored? – Mumbai ITAT sends gold stock valuation addition back for fresh examination
summary: The ITAT restored the ₹1.36 crore stock valuation addition to the AO after finding that the assessee’s Weighted Average Cost method under AS-2 and ICDS-II was never properly examined. The Tribunal directed fresh verification of inventory valuation records. The post Weighted Average Cost method ignored? – Mumbai ITAT sends gold stock valuation addition back for fresh examination appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Mumbai ITAT returned a ₹1.36 crore stock valuation addition to the Assessing Officer for fresh examination, citing that the assessee’s Weighted Average Cost method under AS-2 and ICDS-II was not properly reviewed. This domestic tax case concerns inventory valuation principles for income tax purposes in India, rather than transfer pricing.

title: ITAT Deletes TP Addition as Nokia Shutdown Led to Distress Sale of Finished Goods
summary: The Chennai ITAT held that transfer pricing benchmarking cannot ignore extraordinary business circumstances arising from the shutdown of a major customer. The Tribunal upheld deletion of TP adjustment after accepting that sale of goods to the AE was a distress sale triggered by Nokia India’s closure. The post ITAT Deletes TP Addition as Nokia Shutdown Led to Distress Sale of Finished Goods appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Chennai ITAT deleted a transfer pricing addition, ruling that benchmarking must consider extraordinary business circumstances, such as the shutdown of a major customer like Nokia India. The Tribunal accepted that the sale of finished goods to an Associated Enterprise (AE) was a distress sale, thereby impacting the arm's length determination.

title: Mumbai ITAT quashes reassessment for AY 2015-16 – AO’s “artificial inflation” of escaped income held legally unsustainable
summary: The Tribunal held that the AO wrongly aggregated actual property value and stamp duty valuation of the same transaction to invoke extended limitation under Section 149(1)(b). The reassessment notice for AY 2015-16 was declared time-barred and without jurisdiction. The post Mumbai ITAT quashes reassessment for AY 2015-16 – AO’s “artificial inflation” of escaped income held legally unsustainable appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Mumbai ITAT quashed a reassessment notice for AY 2015-16, ruling that the Assessing Officer's aggregation of actual property value and stamp duty valuation to inflate escaped income was legally unsustainable. The Tribunal declared the reassessment notice time-barred and without jurisdiction, focusing on general income tax procedural issues rather than transfer pricing.

title: Section 194C TDS Cannot Create Tax Liability on Dissolved Firm if Receipts Already Offered to Tax
summary: The ITAT Delhi held that contractual receipts reflected in the PAN of a dissolved partnership firm could not be taxed again when they were already disclosed in the proprietorship concern of the surviving partner. The Tribunal ruled that such addition would amount to double taxation. The post Section 194C TDS Cannot Create Tax Liability on Dissolved Firm if Receipts Already Offered to Tax appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The ITAT Delhi ruled that contractual receipts of a dissolved partnership firm cannot be taxed again if already disclosed by the surviving partner's proprietorship concern, citing double taxation. This decision clarifies the application of Section 194C TDS provisions for dissolved entities, focusing on general income tax principles rather than transfer pricing.

title: ITAT Chennai Allowed Section 80-IA Deduction as Consumer Tariff Held Correct Benchmark
summary: The Tribunal found that the transfer pricing adjustment was incorrectly computed using SEB sale rates. It allowed deduction based on consumer tariff rates. The decision clarifies benchmarking for captive consumption. The post ITAT Chennai Allowed Section 80-IA Deduction as Consumer Tariff Held Correct Benchmark appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The ITAT Chennai allowed a Section 80-IA deduction, ruling that consumer tariff rates were the correct benchmark for captive consumption instead of SEB sale rates. This decision overturned a transfer pricing adjustment, providing crucial clarity on appropriate benchmarking methodology for intra-group transactions in India.

title: Section 271(1)(c) Penalty Cannot Be Imposed for Valuation Differences Without Proof of Inaccuracy: SC
summary: The Supreme Court held that mere differences in property valuation do not amount to furnishing inaccurate particulars. Penalty under Section 271(1)(c) requires proof of deliberate inaccuracy. The ruling clarifies that estimation disputes alone cannot justify penalty. The post Section 271(1)(c) Penalty Cannot Be Imposed for Valuation Differences Without Proof of Inaccuracy: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: India's Supreme Court ruled that penalties under Section 271(1)(c) cannot be imposed solely on differences in property valuation without proof of deliberate inaccuracy. This decision clarifies that mere estimation disputes do not justify penalties, a principle that may be indirectly relevant to transfer pricing disputes involving valuation.

title: Income Tax: Madras HC Orders Fresh Review Due to Ignored Remand Report Evidence
summary: The Court held that failure to consider a remand report acknowledging agricultural income constituted an apparent error, leading to recall of the earlier judgment and remand for fresh adjudication. The post Income Tax: Madras HC Orders Fresh Review Due to Ignored Remand Report Evidence appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: The Madras High Court ordered a fresh review in an income tax case, recalling an earlier judgment due to the failure to consider a remand report acknowledging agricultural income. The decision highlights procedural fairness in tax adjudication, but it does not directly relate to transfer pricing matters.

title: TP Adjustments Reworked – ITAT Strikes Down Wrong Comparables, Demands Parity & Scientific Analysis
summary: TP ruling clarifies that comparables must meet uniform filters, FAR analysis prevails over search matrix, ESOP adjustments require parity, working capital needs scientific computation, and delayed receivables attract LIBOR-based interest. The post TP Adjustments Reworked – ITAT Strikes Down Wrong Comparables, Demands Parity & Scientific Analysis appeared first on TaxGuru .
ai_summary: An ITAT ruling on transfer pricing adjustments emphasized that comparables must meet uniform filters and that FAR analysis should prevail over simple search matrices. The decision also provided guidance on ESOP adjustments, scientific computation for working capital, and the application of LIBOR-based interest for delayed receivables in intra-group transactions.
